output_text:
Revised the paragraph to eliminate pervasive redundancy (tautological definitions, repeated lists, and restated claims), tightening it by roughly 40% while preserving every substantive fact.

---

**Revised version:**

In this section we describe MolRoute, a benchmark for evaluating multi-LLM routing systems on drug discovery tasks. MolRoute provides a standardized evaluation protocol for routing-based systems applied to molecular property prediction and related workflows.

The benchmark covers three task families: ADMET property prediction, retrosynthesis planning, and molecular question answering. The ADMET subset spans solubility, permeability, and toxicity endpoints. The retrosynthesis subset draws single-step problems from USPTO-50K, where the router must dispatch each query to one of several candidate models. The molecular QA subset consists of property-focused questions that test whether the router can direct queries to the appropriate specialist.

The routing pool contains seven candidate LLMs spanning two categories: general-purpose models (GPT-4, Claude) and chemistry-specialized models (Galactica and a fine-tuned Llama variant). This mix lets us assess whether routers can effectively choose between generalists and specialists for drug discovery tasks.

Our headline metric is cost-adjusted accuracy — accuracy divided by per-query cost in dollars — which captures the accuracy/cost trade-off practitioners face in deployment. We also report raw accuracy and raw cost separately.

Preliminary results show that learned routers outperform fixed-model baselines on cost-adjusted accuracy by 18–24%, and this gain is consistent across all three task families.

---

**Key changes:**
- Collapsed the three-sentence definitional opener ("benchmark we constructed… benchmark we call MolRoute… benchmark designed to evaluate…") into one.
- Removed the ADMET tautology ("solubility, permeability, and toxicity… namely solubility, permeability, and toxicity").
- Cut the duplicated "general-purpose and chemistry-specialized" framing that appeared three times in one paragraph.
- Deduplicated the two cost-adjusted-accuracy justifications into a single clause.
- Removed the closing restatement that "this is consistent across ADMET, retrosynthesis, and molecular QA" since those three families were just named.

**Uncertainty:** I assumed "GPT-4" and "Claude" are intentional names rather than placeholders for specific versions (e.g., GPT-4o, Claude 3.5 Sonnet); if the paper requires versioned model identifiers, those should be specified. I also did not verify the 18–24% figure or the USPTO-50K characterization against the underlying experiments.
